title: No Arc / Machine Won't Weld
description: Diagnose why the welding machine produces no arc or won't weld

start.question: What happens when you try to weld?

options.label: Machine powers on but nothing happens when I trigger
I hear a click/hum but no arc

check_120v.title: No Power on 120V
check_120v.fix:
1. Verify the outlet is live — plug in another device to test.
2. Check that the power cord is fully seated in the machine's inlet.
3. Check the circuit breaker on the outlet's circuit.
4. The OmniPro 220 requires a dedicated 20A circuit on 120V.
5. Don't use extension cords — they cause voltage drop.
6. Check the machine's internal circuit breaker (reset button on back panel).

check_240v.title: No Power on 240V
check_240v.fix:
1. Verify the 240V outlet is energized — test with a multimeter if available.
2. Check that you're using the correct plug adapter (NEMA 6-50 or 14-50).
3. Check the breaker on the panel — 240V circuits use double-pole breakers.
4. Inspect the power cord for damage.
5. The OmniPro 220 requires a 30A or 50A 240V circuit for full output.
6. Check the machine's internal circuit breaker (reset button on back panel).

mig_no_output.question: Do you hear the wire feed motor when you pull the trigger?

options.label: Yes — motor runs but no arc

mig_trigger_check.title: MIG Gun Trigger Not Activating
mig_trigger_check.fix:
1. Check that the gun connector is fully inserted and locked into the machine.
2. Verify the correct process is selected (MIG or Flux-Cored, not Stick/TIG).
3. Try disconnecting and reconnecting the gun.
4. Check the trigger cable for damage.
5. If using a spool gun, ensure it's selected in the machine settings.

mig_ground_check.title: MIG — Motor Runs But No Arc
mig_ground_check.fix:
1. Check work clamp connection — it must be firmly attached to bare metal.
2. Remove paint, rust, or coating at the clamp point.
3. Verify the work clamp cable is plugged into the correct terminal.
4. Check polarity — MIG solid wire uses DCEP (work clamp to negative terminal).
5. Make sure the wire is actually reaching the contact tip — remove the nozzle and check.
6. Replace the contact tip if worn or clogged.

stick_tig_no_output.title: Stick/TIG — No Output
stick_tig_no_output.fix:
1. Verify the correct process is selected on the machine (Stick or TIG).
2. Check that cables are in the correct terminals for your polarity.
3. For Stick: electrode holder to positive, work clamp to negative (DCEP) for most rods.
4. For TIG: torch to negative, work clamp to positive (DCEN).
5. Verify the amperage is set high enough for your electrode/tungsten size.
6. Check that the work clamp has a good connection to clean metal.
7. For TIG: ensure the foot pedal or torch trigger is connected and functioning.

click_no_arc.title: Relay Clicks But No Arc
click_no_arc.fix:
1. The click means the machine is receiving the trigger signal.
2. Check all cable connections — both output terminals and work clamp.
3. Verify polarity is correct for your process.
4. For MIG: check that wire is protruding from the contact tip.
5. Make sure the workpiece is conductive (not painted, coated, or insulated).
6. Try increasing the output settings (voltage/amperage).
7. If all connections are correct, the machine may have an internal issue — contact service.

arc_dies.question: How quickly does the arc die?

options.label: After a few seconds of welding

arc_immediate_die.title: Arc Dies Immediately
arc_immediate_die.fix:
1. Amperage/voltage is too low — increase your settings.
2. For Stick: use a fast striking motion and pull back slightly once arc starts.
3. For MIG: increase wire feed speed — the wire is melting faster than it feeds.
4. Check work clamp connection — intermittent contact causes arc drops.
5. For TIG: check gas flow — contaminated tungsten extinguishes the arc quickly.
arc_immediate_die.reference: See manual Selection Chart for correct settings

arc_thermal.title: Arc Dies After a Few Seconds — Possible Thermal Overload
arc_thermal.fix:
1. Check the thermal overload indicator on the machine display.
2. If the machine overheated, wait for the fan to cool it down (10-15 minutes).
3. You may be exceeding the duty cycle — check the duty cycle chart.
4. At 120V, the machine has reduced output and lower duty cycle.
5. Consider switching to 240V for higher duty cycle ratings.
6. If it's not thermal, check for loose cable connections causing intermittent contact.
